topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt like physical weights pressing down on my chest. I stood near a towering canvas, my hands clenched so tightly my knuckles were white. Each brushstroke, every vibrant splash of color, belonged to me, a direct translation of the chaos and quietude inside my own mind. Now, it was on display, a silent testament to my existence, yet utterly divorced from my will. A woman with an air of practiced nonchalance drifted past, pausing only to tilt her head, a faint, knowing smile playing on her lips before continuing on her way. I felt a sudden, sharp prickle behind my eyes, a desperate urge to simply dissolve into the polished floor. I mumbled something incoherent under my breath, the sound swallowed instantly by the hushed reverence of the room, and quickly turned away, my retreat a jerky, panicked movement.

[story 2]

"This piece is certainly... arresting," a smooth, overly saccharine voice drifted from the crowd. I watched from the periphery as people clustered around my work, their murmurs sounding like judgment whispered in a crowded hall. My breath hitched; it was a shallow, rapid thing, catching painfully in my throat. I wanted to scream, to demand that these vibrant textures, these captured moments of my soul, be returned to the solitude of my studio. My gaze darted around, seeking an escape route, finding only more faces—some curious, some dismissive. I felt a flush creep up my neck, hot and undeniable, and I instinctively pulled my jacket tighter around myself, as if the fabric could somehow shield me from the scrutiny.

[story 3]

I leaned against a cool marble pillar, trying to anchor myself against the swirling tide of attention. The sheer volume of people observing my creations felt overwhelming, a physical pressure against my skin. Every glance felt like a probe, dissecting the very essence of the work I poured into it. When a critic approached, their tone was overly complimentary, a performance I couldn't help but feel mocked by. I forced a polite nod, my jaw tight, focusing intensely on a small imperfection in the painting—a tiny shadow that felt magnified tenfold under the harsh spotlights. A tremor ran through my hand as I gripped my own arm, a silent acknowledgment of the profound sense of exposure settling deep within my core.
